coredata.dc:title: Development of sustainable fluorescence approach with red emissive carbon dots derived from Grewia asiatica fruit for the detection of quinalphos
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Vadia, Foziya Yusuf
Jha, Sanjay
Mehta, Vaibhavkumar N.
Park, Tae Jung
Malek, Naved I.
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: A sustainable and eco-friendly approach was developed for the fabrication of red emissive carbon dots via microwave-assisted method using Grewia asiatica fruits as a source. The as-prepared Grewia asiatica-carbon dots (GA-CDs) exhibited red emission at 672 nm upon the excitation at 420 nm with a quantum yield of 21 %. The characteristic emission intensity of GA-CDs (λEm = 672 nm) is selectively quenched by quinalphos (QLP) pesticide via inner filter effect. The as-prepared GA-CDs serve as a versatile turn-off fluorescent probe for QLP sensing, displaying wider linear range (0.25–60 μM) with an impressive detection limit of 0.00159 μM. The integration of GA-CDs with fluorescence spectrometry offers to establish a rapid, simple and miniaturize analytical strategy for QLP assay in vegetable, water and soil samples. Furthermore, the bioimaging and cytotoxicity characteristics of GA-CDs were evaluated on yeast cells and lung cancer cells, respectively. This method provides a facile and convenient pathway for the on-site detection of QLP in food and agriculture samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Carbon dots
Quinalphos
Analytical method
Inner filter effect
Bio imaging
Cytotoxicity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India under the Women Scientist Scheme-A(DST/ WOS - A/CS-77/2021 ). FYV, NIM , and SKK sincerely acknowledge the Director, SVNIT, Surat, India, for providing the necessary infrastructure to carry out this work. SKK, SJ and VNM thank Gujarat State Biotechnology Mission, Government of Gujarat for providing financial support (Ref. No: GSBTM/JD (R&D)/662/2022-23/00292169, dated 10.03.2023) to this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–visible, fluorescence excitation (λEx: 420 nm) and emission (λEm: 672 nm) spectra of GA-CDs. Inset: Photograph of GA-CDs in daylight and under UV light. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of GA-CDs under λEx (350 – 450 nm).
(a) XPS curves of GA-CDs, and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, and (d) O 1 s.

HR-TEM monograph of (a) GA-CDs (scale bar: 10 nm), (b) GA-CDs with QLP (scale bar: 20 nm), histogram for (c) GA-CDs, (d) GA-CDs with QLP. (e) High-resolution TEM image of GA-CDs (scale bar-2 nm), Inset: lattice fringes of GA-CDs, and (f) XRD analysis of GA-CDs.

(a) Emission spectra of GA-CDs after adding various pesticide solutions (dichlorvos, chlorpyrifos, QLP, malathion, terbufos, profenofos, and temephos, 1000 µM), and (b) digital image of GA-CDs solutions after adding the above pesticides under UV light of 365 nm.

Emission spectra of GA-CDs (a) after adding various concentrations of QLP (0.25–200 µM), (b) photographic image of GA-CDs after adding QLP (0.25 – 200 µM) under 365 nm of UV light, and (c) curve plotted between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I) and concentrations of QLP (0.25–200 μM). (Inset: Calibration graph plotted between the ratio (I0/I) of GA-CDs and concentrations of QLP (0.25–60 µM).

Confocal microscopy image of S. cerevisiae cells in the presence of GA-CDs (scale- 20 µm), (a) bright field, (b) upon excitation at 561 nm, and (c) biocompatibility of GA-CDs was evaluated on A549 cell lines via MTT test.
Schematic demonstration of the synthesis of GA-CDs from Grewia asiatica fruits via microwave method for fluorescence sensing of quinalphos.
Comparison of the analytical performance of GA-CDs integrated fluorescence approach for QLP detection with previously reported methods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: (p-NA- DTC-Au NPs = p-nitroaniline dithiocarbamate capped gold nanoparticles, OPCD@UiO-66-NH2 composite = orthophenylenediammine@UiO-66-NH2, Trypsin-Au-Ag NCs = trypsin encapsulated gold-silver bimetallic nanocluster, ATA-AuNPs = 2-amino-4-thiazole acetic acid anchored AuNPs, SiCNPs-GCE- silicon carbide nanoparticle-coated glassy carbon electrode, SPE−dcCL-EIA=solid-phase extraction (SPE) and direct competitive chemiluminescence enzyme immunoassay, PET immunoassay = photoinduced electron-transfer immunoassay, GC–MS=gas chromatography coupled to mass spectrometry, CL assay = chemiluminescence assay, HPTLC=high-performance thin-layer chromatography).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence determination of QLP in food and environmental samples by using GA-CDs as a fluorescent probe.

head.ce:title: Development of sustainable fluorescence approach with red emissive carbon dots derived from Grewia asiatica fruit for the detection of quinalphos

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sustainable and eco-friendly approach was developed for the fabrication of red emissive carbon dots via microwave-assisted method using Grewia asiatica fruits as a source. The as-prepared Grewia asiatica-carbon dots (GA-CDs) exhibited red emission at 672 nm upon the excitation at 420 nm with a quantum yield of 21 %. The characteristic emission intensity of GA-CDs (λEm = 672 nm) is selectively quenched by quinalphos (QLP) pesticide via inner filter effect. The as-prepared GA-CDs serve as a versatile turn-off fluorescent probe for QLP sensing, displaying wider linear range (0.25–60 μM) with an impressive detection limit of 0.00159 μM. The integration of GA-CDs with fluorescence spectrometry offers to establish a rapid, simple and miniaturize analytical strategy for QLP assay in vegetable, water and soil samples. Furthermore, the bioimaging and cytotoxicity characteristics of GA-CDs were evaluated on yeast cells and lung cancer cells, respectively. This method provides a facile and convenient pathway for the on-site detection of QLP in food and agriculture samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Quinalphos (QLP) is a widely used insecticide of organophosphorus group of pesticide. It is extensively applied on various crops e.g., tea, sugarcane, rice, and different types of citrus fruits, thereby controlling insecticides and improving crop yields [1]. The QLP intoxication can occur through ingestion, inhalation, or skin absorption, leading to cause several diseases such as hypernatraemia, pancreatic cancer, kidney failure, faintness, and impairment of learning and memory abilities [2]. Furthermore, it is categorized as one of the highly hazardous to humans, showing significant effect on the central nervous system by phosphorylating the acetylcholinesterase enzyme, which results to inhibit the enzyme activity. In view of this, the World Health Organization (WHO) has listed QLP as one of the hazardous pesticides [3]. However, it is utilized widely by farmers to control various insects, allowing to improve crop yields, which can also cause to contaminate water, air and soil. Due to the potential health risks associated with QLP, there is a great demand to establish a rapid, facile and miniaturized analytical strategy for QLP assaying in food and environmental samples. In view of this, various analytical techniques including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4], enzyme-linked UV–visible spectroscopy [5], gas chromatography (GC) [6] and fluorescence spectroscopic [7] have been employed for the quantification of QLP in various matrices. There are no doubts on the analytical performances of HPLC and GC techniques, however they have some limitations such as requirement of large volumes of solvents and skilled persons, expensive instrumentation, tedious sample preparations, and inability for on-site assay. Looking into the above limitations, there is an urgent need to introduce a facile and selective nanoprobe for assaying QLP in real samples.

In the current era of research, fluorescent nanomaterials are considered promising nanoprobes for assaying of molecular and ionic species due to their prominent optical properties (high quantum yields, tunable emission and good biocompatibility) [8]. Among fluorescent nanoprobes, carbon dots (CDs) are considered as desirable and eco-friendly fluorescent probes for molecular recognitions as compared to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots because of their inherent remarkable properties (water dispersability, high quantum yield (QY), multi-color emission, and biocompatibility), and simple and cost-effective approaches for their preparations [9–12]. All of these attractive outstanding properties of CDs make them as ideal and eco-friendly fluorescent nanostructure materials in various fields including optoelectronics, drug delivery, fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting, sensors, cellular imaging and photocatalysis [13–15]. In the past few decades, numerous synthetic approaches (electrochemical synthesis, laser irradiation, hydro/solvothermal, microwave irradiation and chemical oxidation) have been adopted for fabrication of CDs, exhibiting promising applications in bioimaging and analytical chemistry [16]. Among these synthetic approaches, microwave irradiation has become a promising method for synthesizing fluorescent CDs because of its simplicity and rapidity [17]. Using microwave irradiation, CDs have been successfully synthesized by using numerous carbon sources and demonstrated for fluorescence sensing and cell imaging applications [17,18]. In order to familiarize microwave approach for the fabrication of CDs, several plant-based materials such as bauhinia flower [19], lemon and onion juices [20], rose flower [21], kelp [22], calotropis gigantea [23], and carrageenan [24] have been utilized as precursors for the preparation of CDs. Noticeably, the as-prepared CDs displayed blue/green fluorescence by using the above carbon sources. Moreover, blue fluorescent CDs were prepared from Averrhoa bilimbi fruit for Cu2+ ion and QLP assays via turn-off–on approach [25]. To alter the fluorescence emission (blue/green to red) in CDs, Grewia asiatica (GA) (Phalsa) fruits are chosen as green precursor for synthesizing GA-CDs with red fluorescence for pesticide sensing application. Since Grewia asiatica (Phalsa) fruits contain several bioactive phytochemicals i.e., phenolic compounds, amino acids, tannins, organic acids, flavonoids, and anthocyanins, exhibiting potential pharmacological applications (anticancer, anti-inflammatory, high antioxidant, antidiabetic, antimicrobial, hepatoprotective and radioprotective activity) [26]. These phytochemicals play an essential role in fabricating CDs, which can alter their fluorescence emission. Inspired by the above studies, red fluorescent CDs are prepared from fruits of Grewia asiatica (GA) (Phalsa) via microwave method and integrated with fluorescence spectrometry for the detection of QLP.

Herein, a facile and eco-friendly synthetic approach was introduced for synthesizing CDs with red emission from Grewia asiatica fruits via microwave method (Scheme 1 ). The as-fabricated GA-CDs exhibited an intensive emission peak at 672 nm (excited at 420 nm). Remarkably, QLP drastically quench the red emission of GA-CDs via inner filter effect (IFE). It was observed that the quenching of red fluorescence of GA-CDs by QLP displays good linearity (0.25–60 μM) with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.00159 μM (1.59 nM) for QLP. The GA-CDs integrated fluorescence approach was effectively implemented to QLP assay in food and environmental samples. Furthermore, the prepared GA-CDs showed good biocompatible nature for lung cancer (A549) cell lines and acted as probes for imaging of Saccharomyces cerevisiae (yeast) cells, respectively.

Grewia asiatica (Phalsa) spoiled fruit was obtained from the local shop, Surat, Gujarat. Each chemical utilized in the experiment is of analytical grade and employed directly without any further purification. Quinalphos and chlorpyrifos were collected from Gujarat Insecticides Limited. Malathion, terbufos, and profenofos were gifted from Cormandel International Limited and Cheminova India limited, respectively. Dichlorvos and temephos were received from Atul Limited. Ethanol was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Acetonitrile and dimethyl formamide (DMF) were bought from SRL Chemicals, India. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was purchased from Finar Chemicals, India.

Different analytical instruments were used to characterize the synthesized GA-CDs. UV-absorption spectral studies were performed on a Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer, Ocean Optics, USA. The fluorescence spectral characteristics of GA-CDs were recorded on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer, Agilent Technologies, USA. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry was utilized to measure the surface functionalities of GA-CDs (FT-IR APHA II, Bruker, Germany). The surface charge and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis is performed by using Horiba SZ-100.

Grewia asiatica fruits were cleaned with water and removed the seeds, the remaining fruit pulp was transferred to a beaker. Then, 10 mL of EtOH was added to the pulp and the mixture was subjected to sonication (20 min). The solution was then heated for 15 min at 600 W in a domestic microwave oven. The obtained residue was dispersed in water followed by sonicated for 15 min and then filtered using J-SIL Nylon syringe filter (pore size 0.45 µm). Lastly, the obtained solution of as-prepared GA-CDs was stored at 5 °C. Scheme 1 represents the fabrication of GA-CDs and describes their potential application.

The emission efficiency of GA-CDs was evaluated by comparing it with rhodamine B (RhB), which has a known fluorescence QY of 0.31. To determine the QY of GA-CDs, the integrated fluorescence emission peak (excited at 420 nm) and absorbance values (at 305 nm for GA-CDs) were measured relative to those of RhB, using the following equation.
Q
GA - C D s =
Q
RhB
×
I
GA - C D s
/
I R
×
η
GA - C D s
/
η R
×
A R
/
A
GA - C D s
where QGA-CDs signifies the QY of GA-CDs, QRhB represents the QY of reference (QRhB=0.31 in H2O), “I” denotes an integration area of GA-CDs and RhB’s emission peak, “η” represents GA-CDs and RhB’s refractive index, subscript “R” is reference sample RhB, “A” is the absorbance of GA-CDs and RhB. The calculated QY of GA-CDs is 21 %.

To establish the fluorescence turn-off approach for detecting QLP, the as-fabricated GA-CDs (1.0 mL) solutions were treated with different pesticides (dichlorvos, chlorpyrifos, quinalphos, malathion, terbufos, profenofos, and temephos, 1.0 mM, 0.5 mL). Then, the solutions were thoroughly vortexed for 60 s and allowed to stand for 2 min and their spectra were measured by a fluorescence spectrometer from 600 to 800 nm under λEx = 420 nm. In order to estimate the sensitivity of the probe, the emission intensities were measured at least three time for constructing calibration graph for the quantification of QLP.

The GA-CDs were utilized as a fluorescent probe for imaging applications. The yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae (S. cerevisiae) cells treated with with GA-CDs at a concentration of 20 ng/mL and the fluorescence images of S. cerevisiae cells were measured by using fluorescence microscopy. To examine the biocompatibility of GA-CDs, cytotoxicity study was performed on A549 human lung adenocarcinoma cells by methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay. Briefly, cells were plated at 10,000 cell density per well (in 96-well plates) and then exposed to different concentrations of GA-CDs (7.8–250 ng/m). Cell cycle was evaluated by treating both cells and control wells with MTT solution (5 µL) for 90 min at 37 °C. The cytotoxicity of GA-CDs was evaluated by measuring λmax at 570 and 670 nm.

To get the optimum fluorescence intensity, GA-CDs were synthesized from Grewia asiatica fruits by applying different watt (300–800 W) and time (5–20 min) parameters using a domestic microwave oven (Supporting Information, Fig. S1a–b). It can be observed that the best emission spectral data of GA-CDs were noticed by using microwave power of 600 W and reaction time of 15 min for the formation of GA-CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GA-CDs showed the absorbance peak at 305 nm suggesting the existence of n-π* and π-π* transitions, which confirms the existence of C=O, C-O, and C=C on GA-CDs (Fig. 1 a). In Fig. 1b, the fluorescence spectra of GA-CDs showed the emission peak at 672 nm, which is independent of λEx in the range of 350 – 450 nm, displaying the highest emission intensity when excited at 420 nm. Therefore, the λEm,max of GA-CDs was noticed at 672 nm when λEm at 420 nm (Fig. 1b). The as-fabricated GA-CDs possess intense red fluorescence under 365 nm of UV light, and yellowish color in day light (Inset of Fig. 1). Moreover, the confirmation of GA-CDs formation was achieved through the analysis of fluorescence and absorption spectra of GA-CDs and extract of Grewia asiatica, as presented in Supporting Information, Fig. S2a–b. The spectral data indicated that the distinct differences between the spectral characteristics of as-prepared GA-CDs andextract of Grewia asiatica, which confirms the formation of GA-CDs. The stability of GA-CDs was estimated by examining the fluorescence emission spectra at different time intervals, which suggests that the stability of the as-prepared GA-CDs is stable up to 7 days, after that the emission peak intensity of GA-CDs is progressively decreased over time due to the aggregation of GA-CDs as shown in Supporting Information, Fig. S3. Overall, the as-fabricated GA-CDs displayed good stability that can be utilized for fluorescence sensing application.

The FT-IR analysis was employed to identify functional groups existing on the surface of as-prepared GA-CDs. To this, FT-IR spectra of GA-CDs and extract of Grewia asiatica were studied and shown in Supporting Information, Fig. S4. A broad peak was detected at 3445 cm−1 that belongs to the –OH group stretching, but, in the FT-IR spectrum of GA-CDs, this peak was shifted to 3398 cm−1. Similarly, C–H and C=O groups stretching were observed at 2930 and 1724 cm−1, respectively. A distinctive peak at 1634 cm−1 is attributed to –NH2 bending. The peaks at 1403 and 1247 cm−1 are ascribed to –C–H bending and C–O stretching, respectively. It can be revealed that the characteristic peaks of C–H, C=O, C–O stretching, and –NH2, C–H bending, are either slightly shifted or decreased the intensity, confirming the formation of GA-CDs. The XPS study was also carried out to confirm the elemental composition of GA-CDs. The as-prepared GA-CDs contain carbon (28.21 %), oxygen (54.73 %), nitrogen (3.42 %), and sodium (13.63 %), confirming that heteroatom (nitrogen) is also present in GA-CDs, which can alter the fluorescence properties of CDs. The survey XPS spectrum of GA-CDs displayed three diverse peaks at 284.5, 533.2 and 399.5 eV, which correspond to C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s elements, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The XPS spectrum of C 1 s displayed two peaks at 284.4 and 287.7 eV (Fig. 2b), which are ascribed to C=C (sp2 carbon) and C–O and C=O groups [27]. The N 1 s XPS spectrum exhibited a peak at 399.5 eV (Fig. 2c), which belongs to the tertiary nitrogen N-(C)3 groups. In Fig. 2d, the O 1 s XPS spectrum exhibited two peaks at 531.2 and 534.4 eV correspond to the COO– and O=C–O groups, respectively suggesting the presence of carbonyl and hydroxyl group on the surface of GA-CDs [28]. Moreover, the peaks at 1072 and 498 eV in the survey spectrum of GA-CDs correspond to the Na 1 s and its Auger electron energy, respectively [29].

Furthermore, the HR-TEM analysis was employed to investigate the size and morphology of GA-CDs, which reveal that the as-fabricated GA-CDs are very small and nearly spherical shape nanostructures. The HR-TEM monograph demonstrated uniform carbon nanostructures with a mean diameter of 6.83 ± 1.94 nm (Fig. 3 a and c) for GA-CDs. The HR-TEM image of GA-CDs displays the lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing (d) of 0.20 nm (Fig. 3e) [30]. Moreover, Fig. 3(f) illustrates the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of GA-CDs, revealing a prominent peak at approximately 2θ = 28.4°, which is an indicative of (002) diffraction pattern of graphitic carbon. Additionally, a less obvious peak is observed at around 2θ = 40.6°, which corresponds to (001) diffraction pattern of graphitic carbon [31]. In addition, the average hydrodynamic diameter of the fabricated GA-CDs was investigated by DLS analysis, indicating that GA-CDs had a diameter of 8.1 nm as depicted in Supporting Information, Fig. S5a. The zeta potential study of the synthesized GA-CDs showed that they possess a negative surface charge, with a measured value of −25.4 mV (Supporting Information, Fig. S6a). This observation indicates that anionic surface on GA-CDs is likely ascribed to the existence of oxygen-containing groups, such as –OH and C=O groups. The above analytical data confirm that the formation of red emissive GA-CDs with a size of <10 nm.

The impact of solvent and pH on the emission characteristics of the as-prepared GA-CDs were assessed by measing the emission spectra since the fluorescence properties of carbon nanostructures are affected by various factors. For the solvent effect, different solvents such as water, ethanol, DMF, acetonitrile, and DMSO were used and investigated their emission spectral characteristics. The as-prepared GA-CDs were dispersed in the solvents mentioned above and the spectra were measured by applying λEx at 420 nm (Supporting Information, Fig. S7a). The spectral data revealed that the fluorescence intensity of GA-CDs varies using different solvents due to the various emissive defects on GA-CDs surface. Moreover, the stability of GA-CDs in the above solvents was examined by measuring the spectra after 15 days (Supporting Information, Fig. S7(b)), revealing that the fluorescence intensity was remarkably decreased in the solvents (EtOH, acetonitrile, DMF and DMSO), whereas the fluorescence intensity of GA-CDs does not change by dispersing GA-CDs in water, which confirms that the as-prepared GA-CDs exhibit good stability and emission properties using water as a sovlent. Hence, the as-prepared GA-CDs are not stable in other solvents (EtOH, acetonitrile, DMF and DMSO) and the entire experiment was accomplished by dissolving GA-CDs in water. In addition, the effect of different phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) pH levels on QLP detection using GA-CDs (Supporting Information, Fig. S8a-b). Initially, the effect of PBS pH levels (2.0 – 12.0) on the emission spectra of GA-CDs was examined without QLP, showing the emission intensity of GA-CDs does not show any significant changes at different PBS pH levels. Moreover, the impact of PBS pH on the fluorescence quenching of GA-CDs by QLP was investigated across PBS pH (2.0–12.0). The emission spectral data indicated that the quenching of the emission intensity by QLP is almost similar with PBS pH and without pH, suggesting QLP assay could be carried out by using GA-CDs as a probe without any buffer pH.

In order to introduce red emissive GA-CDs as a sensor in analytical chemistry, the fluorescence behavior of red emissive GA-CDs was investigated in response to different pesticides solutions (dichlorvos, chlorpyrifos, QLP, malathion, terbufos, profenofos, and temephos, 1.0 mM), signifying that only QLP significantly quenched the intensity of GA-CDs while other pesticides did not show any change (Fig. 4 a). Moreover, the above pesticides solutions treated with GA-CDs solutions in 5 mL glass vials exposed to UV light at 365 nm, revealing only QLP-GA-CDs solution red fluorescence was faded, whereas the red fluorescence color of GA-CDs solutions with other pesticides remain stable (Fig. 4b), which confirms that GA-CDs act as a sensor for QLP detection. To define the kinetic characteristics of the interaction between GA-CDs and QLP, the time-dependent fluorescence responses of GA-CDs were measured upon addition of QLP (1000 µM) [32,33] (Supporting Information, Fig. S9). These results demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching of GA-CDs by QLP was varied with incubation time (1–30 min). Initially, the fluorescence intensity was decreased dramatically, then stabilized, reaching a plateau after 10 min, indicating that the interaction between GA-CDs and QLP had reached equilibrium.

To ascertain the analytical performance of GA-CDs-based fluorescent sensor, the QLP concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching of GA-CDs was evaluated and the spectral data were shown in Fig. 5 a. When different concentrations of QLP (0.25–200 μM) was added independently to GA-CDs solutions, it promoted the quench of emission intensity of GA-CDs. With the increasing concentration of QLP, the emission intensity at 672 nm decreases gradually and the visual fluorescence change of GA-CDs solutions with different concentrations of QLP can be monitored under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 5b), showing gradual decrese in the red fluorescence of GA-CDs with increasing QLP concentration, which is in good agreement with the quenching intensity of GA-CDs. To further investigate the sensing mechanism, Stern-Volmer equation (I0/I=1 + Ksv[Q]) was employed. By plotting the fluorescence emission intensity ratio (I0/I) at 672 nm against the QLP concentration [Q], a favorable linear correlation was observed within the range of 0.25–200 μM (Fig. 5c). Using Stern-Volmer equation, the quenching constant (Ksv) was calculated to be 0.11 μM−1. This quenching constant provides quantitative insights into the efficiency of the fluorescence quenching process between GA-CDs and QLP.

The experimental findings demonstrated that the presence of QLP would specifically quench the emission of GA-CDs. This observation suggests the potential of GA-CDs as a sensitive probe for sensing of QLP. To understand the underlying quenching mechanism, the optical properties of both GA-CDs and QLP were further investigated. Supporting Information, Fig. S10 presents the UV absorption spectrum of QLP alongside the excitation and emission spectra of GA-CDs. Notably, a significant overlap was observed between the UV absorption spectrum of QLP and the excitation spectrum of GA-CDs. This spectral overlap indicates that the probable quenching mechanism of GA-CDs fluorescence by QLP via IFE. The IFE occurs when the analyte (QLP) absorbs the excitation light intended for the fluorescent probe (GA-CDs), thereby reducing the effective excitation of the probe and leading to fluorescence quenching [45,46]. Furthermore, HRTEM images in Fig. 3b and d illustrate a notable increase in the average size of GA-CDs from 6.83 ± 1.94 nm to 14.7 ± 2.47 nm with QLP addition, indicating the aggregation of GA-CDs in the presence of QLP. The hydrodynamic diameters of GA-CDs and GA-CDs with QLP were examined, revealing a notable difference. GA-CDs exhibited a diameter of 8.1 nm, whereas in the presence of QLP, the diameter increased significantly to 19.1 nm (Supporting Information, Fig. S5b). This substantial increase in diameter suggests that the existence of QLP induces a considerable rise in the hydrodynamic size of GA-CDs. The zeta potential of GA-CDs, initially at −25.4 mV, reduces to −9.0 mV upon QLP addition (Supporting Information, Fig. S6b), indicating electrostatic interaction between GA-CDs and QLP. The FT-IR spectra of GA-CDs and GA-CDs + QLP were shown in Supporting Information, Fig. S4b. The findings revealed a reduction in the intensity of the characteristic peaks that were present on the surface of GA-CDs when QLP was introduced to GA-CDs. This observation confirmed that the interaction of QLP with GA-CDs surface. Further, the fluorescence lifetimes of GA-CDs and GA-CDs-QLP were measured, which shows that the GA-CDs had an average fluorescence decay time of 5.57 ns. However, upon the addition of QLP, the fluorescence lifetime of GA-CDs decreased to 3.40 ns (Supporting Information, Fig. S11). The decrease in the lifetime of GA-CDs by QLP is due to the dynamic quenching process. This is due to the collisional interactions between QLP and GA-CDs at the excited state, leading to non-radiative deactivation of the fluorescence [47]. Also, Ksv of 0.11 μM−1 suggests that a reasonable excited state lifetime that permits efficient quenching interactions, and even low concentrations of QLP are sufficient to significantly reduce the fluorescence intensity of GA-CDs by dynamically. These findings confirm that GA-CDs can effectively function as a fluorescent sensor for QLP detection.

The selectivity of the developed GA-CDs-based nanosensor is crucial in sensing QLP in complex real samples. In this study, the sensing ability of GA-CDs was examined in the presence of some interfering species (metal ions − Hg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, and Cu2+; anions − Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, and Br−; and other pesticides − phenthoate, fipronil, thiamethoxam, and imidacloprid) with and without QLP (Supporting Information, Fig. S12). It was observed that while other pesticides did not significantly affect the fluorescence intensity of GA-CDs, only QLP consistently cause a noticeable decrease in the fluorescence emission intensity of GA-CDs even when other pesticides were present. The characteristic emission spectra of the as-fabricated GA-CDs did not show any significant changes when interfering substances are present, however the emission peak at 672 nm remarkably quenched by QLP even the presence of other species, demonstrating the developed fluorescent probe possesses outstanding anti-interference performance for QLP sensing. Thus, GA-CDs acted as a selective probe for QLP sensing.

Bioimaging and biocompatibility of GA-CDs were evaluated by using yeast and cancer cells. Bioimaging ability of GA-CDs was evaluated on S. cerevisiae cells (Fig. 6 a–b). Briefly, S. cerevisiae cells were incubated with GA-CDs (20 ng/mL) for 1–6 h and measured the fluorescence images (Fig. 6b). It can be noticed that intense red fluorescence was observed in S. cerevisiae cells, suggesting the effective internalization of GA-CDs, which confirms that the as-prepared GA-CDs have potential for bioimaging applications. Furthermore, the cell viability of GA-CDs was estimated on the A549 cell line using MTT test, as shown in Fig. 6c. The findings reveal that the cell viability remained high, ranging from 99.4 % to 97.8 %, across a wide concentration range of GA-CDs from 7.8 to 250 ng/mL. The findings suggest that GA-CDs exhibit biocompatibility for biomedical studies.

To further assess the practical utility of GA-CDs as QLP probes, vegetable samples (cabbage and cauliflower) and fruit samples (apple) were obtained from local vendors in Surat, Gujarat, and quantified QLP by using GA-CDs as a probe. The environmental samples (river water and soil samples) were collected from the Tapi River and agricultural fields in Surat, Gujarat. The food samples were washed with water and chopped. After that, the samples were spiked with the different concentrations (0.25, 2.5, and 10 μM) of QLP and put aside overnight. After that, water and ethanol were added in a 1:1 ratio, and it was sonicated for 30 min. Using GA-CDs as probes, the resultant solution was filtered (Whatman filter paper 292) to measure the spectra. The standard addition recovery experiment was performed with a different QLP concentration (0.25, 2.5, and 10 μM). River water samples were filtered using Whatman filter paper to remove the insoluble substances followed by spiking above concentrations of QLP. For soil sample, 0.5 g of soil was pre-treated by dispersing in 10 mL of water, followed by sonication for 15 min, and centrifuged at 8000 RPM for 15 min. The resulting supernatant was spiked with the above concentrations of QLP and analyzed by the aforesaid procedure. The obtained results demonstrated that the developed GA-CDs-based fluorescence method exhibits good recoveries in the 95.36–99.86 % range with the relative standard deviation (RSD) values below 2 % (Table 2 ). These findings demonstrate that GA-CDs-based fluorescent sensor could be applied for QLP assay in real samples with reduced sample preparations and minimized sample volumes.

To summarize, a straightforward microwave heating process was developed for the synthesis of red emissive GA-CDs using Grewia asiatica fruits as a source. The as-prepared GA-CDs exhibited red emission at 672 nm upon the excitation at 420 nm. The resulting GA-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence emission properties in water with a QY of %, making them ideal as fluorescent probes for detecting QLP via IFE. The as-synthesized GA-CDs-based fluorescence sensor exhibits good linearity in the range of 0.25–60 µM with a LOD of 0.00159 µM for QLP. The analytical performance of GA-CDs-based fluorescence approach was successfully demonstrated for QLP assay in vegetable, water and soil samples, accomplishing with reduced sample preparations and sample volumes, which can favor for on-site sensing of QLP. Moreover, cell imaging and cell viability studies prove that GA-CDs are the potential candidates for bioimaging and biomedical applications. Thus, the as-synthesized GA-CDs could be effectively integrated with fluorescence techniques (spectroscopy and microscopy) for QLP detection and bioimaging applications.

Foziya Yusuf Vadia: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Sanjay Jha: . Vaibhavkumar N. Mehta: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tae Jung Park: . Naved I. Malek: . Suresh Kumar Kailasa: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India under the Women Scientist Scheme-A(DST/WOS-A/CS-77/2021). FYV, NIM, and SKK sincerely acknowledge the Director, SVNIT, Surat, India, for providing the necessary infrastructure to carry out this work. SKK, SJ and VNM thank Gujarat State Biotechnology Mission, Government of Gujarat for providing financial support (Ref. No: GSBTM/JD(R&D)/662/2022-23/00292169, dated 10.03.2023) to this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.115948.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Zhang, C. Zhang, Z. Li, J. Ge, C. Li, C. Dong, S. Shuang, Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent probe for detection of curcumin based on the inner filter effect. RSC Adv. 5(115) (2015) 95054–95060. https://doi.org/10.1039/C5RA18176C.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence immunoassay based on the inner-filter effect of carbon dots for highly sensitive amantadine detection in foodstuffs
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Dong, H. Li, G. Mujtaba Mari, X. Yu, W. Yu, K. Wen, Y. Ke, J. Shen, Z. Wang, Fluorescence immunoassay based on the inner-filter effect of carbon dots for highly sensitive amantadine detection in foodstuffs. Food Chem. 294 (2019) 347–354. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2019.05.082.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational design of niobium carbide MXene quantum dots decorated with arginine for the fluorescence sensing of superoxide anion in Saccharomyces cerevisiae cells
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.J. Joshi, S. Jha, N.I. Malek, T.J. Park, S.K. Kailasa, Rational design of niobium carbide MXene quantum dots decorated with arginine for the fluorescence sensing of superoxide anion in Saccharomyces cerevisiae cells. Sens. Actuators B Chem. 404 (2024) 135226. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2023.135226.
